title: Chapter Seventy: The Barrier Falls
content:
## JEONG YEON-SHIN

The sky cracked.

The first impact had been a ripple -- a shudder that passed through the sorcery formation like a stone dropped into still water. The second was different.

*BOOM!*

Something broke. For one terrible instant, heaven and earth seemed to overturn. The Two Flame Blood Spirit Formation -- the thousand-year barrier that had hidden the Blood Flame Sect's headquarters for centuries -- shattered.

Its destruction felt like the collapse of a world.

"Moon Spirit Divine Technique!" The Blood Flame Sect Leader surged to his feet. The lounging elegance, the amused detachment -- all of it gone. In its place stood something ancient and dangerous, lips torn wide in a snarl.

*BOOM!*

A snow-white storm swept through the compound. Fragments of the shattered barrier whirled like shrapnel, carried on winds that came from everywhere at once.

The Third Apostle's massive energy sense spread outward, tasting the devastation, and what it found broke his composure entirely.

"Step forth!" he roared.

But first the Third Apostle turned his gaze on Yeon-shin. Blood Swordmasters were already rushing forward -- a dozen of them, blades drawn, converging on the young Grand Teacher who had just murdered one of their own. Yeon-shin met them with open hands. The Demon Roaring Blood Technique wrapped around dharma power as he spun, deflecting sword strikes, crushing a swordmaster's temple with his palm, breaking another's elbow at the joint.

The Third Apostle descended the steps. His Demon Roaring Blood Technique burned in every meridian -- vast, ancient cultivation that made the air itself seem to boil.

"I knew your family well," he said, his voice carrying through the chaos with the ease of a man accustomed to being heard above screaming. "Henan's Xinye County. Jeong Family Estate. A worthless third-rate martial house."

Yeon-shin did not answer. He shattered a Blood Swordmaster's jaw and stepped over the body.

"Your father, the Jeong family head, was known as a playboy in Henan. Spread love affairs with all sorts of young ladies. Seduced your mother with his looks. The previous Divine Sword Squad Leader -- that petty old man -- let live the fellow who took his daughter as second wife."

The words were designed to wound. Designed to make him lose focus.

He felt the door inside him rattle.

"You are lowborn because your father was trash. Even if you are Ma Yeon-jeok's grandson."

*Keep it shut.*

"Your obsolete grandfather begged us to spare just you, fearing revenge from Desolate Sect's lord. And you repay our sect's grace with enmity."

The Third Apostle's power bore down on him -- a net of momentum that felt like an invisible prison closing around his body. Supreme cultivation comparable to the Namgung family's Imperial Sword Form.

"One move," the Third Apostle said. "Since you will die the moment you try to block, conduct yourself properly as a lowborn."

That was when the sky broke.

Desolate Sect's lord did not grant them leisure.

She descended from the heavens. One moment she was a figure against the sky; the next she was on the ground -- as if she had seeped into the wind itself and reformed where she pleased.

Light green hair and robes emitting clarity beyond the mortal realm. A precious sword raised at her side, its radiance flowing into her mystical pupils and white skin.

Not a fairy.

The judge of the netherworld.

She took one step. The hem of her grass-colored robes brushed the earth with a soft sound. The sunlight seemed particularly bright.

"Desolate Sect's lord...?" The Third Apostle's gasp was a question. Recognition, delayed by disbelief.

She did not answer.

*Swoosh.*

The Third Apostle opened his mouth to give a battle cry. By the time the sound left his throat, she was before him, delivering a sword strike that rippled like a transparent breeze.

*Thud.*

His head fell. An end unbefitting a notorious supreme master.

Yeon-shin watched with wide eyes. He had emerged from his trance the instant the formation shattered, his upper dantian sense and his physical sight merging into a single terrible clarity.

*I could not see the move.*

He guessed the Third Apostle had not seen it either. Only the death arriving before his eyes.

Absolute master. Peerless master.

She would gain the upper hand against whoever she faced. There was no one in the world to compare.

A deathly silence covered the courtyard.

Then the Blood Flame Sect Leader appeared before her. His blood-colored robe embroidered with a golden dragon fluttered belatedly. He swept his snow-white hair behind his back.

"This is our first meeting."

"I have no words to share with a criminal."

No emotion in her tone.

Her sword moved. Pale light flashed. Blood burst from the Sect Leader's nape.

But he did not die. He had withdrawn half a step -- reacting to her blade, stepping the evasion footwork. The wound on his neck boiled and sealed itself, flesh knitting together with blood technique regeneration that made Yeon-shin's stomach turn.

*Hehe.*

The Sect Leader's bright red lips tore wide on both sides. A phlegm-rattled laugh. A challenge.

Both absolute beings vanished.

*BOOM!*

The collision came from the mountain behind the Sect Leader's hall -- a sound like hundreds of thunderclap bombs detonating at once. Dust rose in an enormous plume.

Then silence. The battle of peerless masters was not continuous bombardment. It was feints and evasions, movements too fast for human eyes, punctuated by collisions that shook the earth.

*One breath between them. An ordinary person's heartbeat is eternity at their level.*

Yeon-shin sealed his ear canals with a true qi barrier and turned his attention to what he could affect.

"Destroy the evil sect!"

"Breaking Evil Manifesting Truth! Demon-Destroying Devil-Eliminating!"

The sounds of sword strikes and energy waves crashed against the compound from all directions. Hundreds of martial artists storming the Blood Flame Sect headquarters through the shattered barrier.

The ones rushing at the front were familiar.

Cheong Myeong, fastest of all -- blue eyes blazing, blade drawn, cutting through Blood Swordmasters with the effortless precision of wind through tall grass.

Wei Ji Myo-hwa behind him. Baek Mi-ryeo. Little Divine Monk Gak Jeong, his body technique matching theirs step for step. Even Mount Hua Sect's Plum Blossom Sword Masters charged in their wake.

Welcome. The sight of them was like finding shore after drowning.

"Young Hero Jeong...!"

That voice.

Won-chang. At the tail end of the vanguard, running with a gaunt face and wild eyes, his lightness technique carrying him past sword strikes that would have taken his head. He saw Yeon-shin and managed a smile -- exhausted, threadbare, real.

The same companion. Still the same.

*You have recovered your martial arts.*

Relief flooded him. Then discipline clamped down. The Sixth Apostle had risen from his seat, killing intent building like a storm front. His lean frame and stern impression radiated danger -- a supreme master comparable to Desolate Sect's black-ranked great masters.

"You were our sect's calamity," the Sixth Apostle said, staring at Yeon-shin.

*There is no supreme master among the allies who can match an Apostle.*

"Desolate Sect's squad leaders are coming!" Cheong Myeong shouted. Empty bravado. Yeon-shin's energy sense had already confirmed: no black-ranked among the allied forces. Desolate Sect's chronic problem -- too few warriors to cover the vast Central Plains.

The compound erupted into chaos. Blood Swordmasters clashed with orthodox martial artists across every level of the courtyard. Steel rang against steel. Bodies fell. Red and blue and white tangled together in a storm of violence.

Won-chang reached him. Breathing hard. Blood on his sleeves that was not his own. His face was thinner than Yeon-shin remembered -- the cheeks hollowed, the jaw sharper. He had not been eating well.

"You are wearing red," Won-chang said. His voice cracked on the second word. His eyes were full.

"Not for much longer."

From the mountain, another earth-shaking collision. The ground trembled beneath their feet. Somewhere beyond the dust, two absolute beings fought at speeds no human eye could track -- each breath between collisions an eternity at their level.

Yeon-shin did not look. He trusted her.

He grabbed the boy Gao Zong-xue had beaten and hauled him upright. The child stared at him with confused, swollen eyes.

"Stay close to that man," Yeon-shin said, pushing him toward Won-chang. "He talks too much, but he will keep you alive."

Won-chang caught the boy. His arms went around the child the way a father holds a son, automatic and fierce. Then he looked at Yeon-shin, and his smile cracked wide, and tears cut tracks through the grime on his face.

"I never stopped searching," he said. "Not for one day. The Sect Lord gathered warriors. Cheong Myeong negotiated with three of the Nine Sects. Even Wei Ji Myo-hwa came. All for you."

*I know.*

He could not say the words. The door inside him -- the one he had kept shut through months of captivity, through the Seventh Apostle's hands on his body and the Third Apostle's taunts about his dead parents and the nightly screams of the drained -- that door shuddered on its hinges.

He kept it shut.

But something in his face must have shown, because Won-chang's expression changed. The older man's tears dried. His jaw set. He gripped Yeon-shin's shoulder -- once, hard, the way sword-brothers do.

"When we are clear," Yeon-shin said quietly, "I want to rest for a while."

Won-chang nodded. Just that. The grip on his shoulder tightened, then released.

The mountain roared again. The sky rippled with energies that did not belong to the mortal world.

Yeon-shin turned toward the chaos. Blood-colored robe fluttering, black hair streaming, the Poison Avoiding Pearl cool in his fist. A month of hidden formations embedded in every stone and railing around him. An escape route he had planned from the first day.

He raised his hands.

*Woong.*

The Demon-Destroying Azure Unyielding Palm, planted across dozens of structures throughout the headquarters, awakened. Energy surged from marble and wood and stone -- a hidden formation within the formation, triggered at last.

The Sect Leader's hall trembled. Pillars cracked. The thirty-six steps of affliction groaned and split.

The Blood Flame Sect masters stumbled, their footing stolen, their coordination shattered. The orthodox martial artists surged forward into the gap.

Yeon-shin lowered his hands.

Through the dust and the screaming and the ring of steel, he walked toward his people.

The sunset was red. The same color as the robe he was already shrugging from his shoulders, peeling it away like a second skin, letting the Pure Blood Robe fall in the dirt behind him.

Beneath it, he wore nothing of Desolate Sect. Those clothes had been confiscated months ago. But Won-chang was already pulling a bundle from his pack -- creased, travel-worn, carefully folded.

Blue martial clothes. The golden thread of the Sect Lord's direct disciples glinted at the shoulders.

"I brought them," Won-chang said simply. "I knew you would need them."

Yeon-shin took the clothes. The Heaven Silkworm fabric was cool against his fingers -- familiar in a way that made his chest tighten. He pulled them on over bloodstained skin.

Blue clothes. That was who he was.

Cheong Myeong appeared at his side, blade wet, expression for once entirely serious. "You have a bad habit of getting captured."

"I have a good habit of getting out."

The Elf's mouth twitched. Almost a smile. Almost.

Wei Ji Myo-hwa cut her way through a knot of Blood Swordmasters and stopped before him. Soot on her face. Blood on her sword. Her eyes moved over him -- the bruises, the thinness, the way he held his weight.

She did not ask if he was alright. She was too wise for that.

"The Sect Lord fights behind the mountain," she said. "We should withdraw before the Sixth Apostle decides to die for his cause."

"Aye," Yeon-shin said.

The mountain fell silent.

And from the ruins of the Blood Flame Sect's thousand-year stronghold, Jeong Yeon-shin walked out into the fading light, his people around him. Won-chang on one side. The beaten boy on the other. Cheong Myeong ahead, blade drawn, clearing the path.

The pressure at his crown pulsed -- once, twice, a hot rhythmic ache that blurred the edges of his vision. He breathed through it.

*Not now. Not yet.*

Behind him, the Pure Blood Robe lay in the dirt where it had fallen. Red and empty and already forgotten.

He did not look back.
